meta.title: WOV Tech s. r. o. | IT infrastructure for small and mid-sized companies
meta.description: We help small and mid-sized companies build clear, stable and secure IT infrastructure. Cloud, monitoring, backup, infrastructure design and IT stabilisation.

home.body:
We help small and mid-sized companies organise and stabilise IT so it supports work and growth

We work with companies that want more clarity, control and confidence in their IT. When infrastructure is messy, unstable or growing without a clear system, we help structure it in a practical and understandable way.

Our main focus is infrastructure design, cloud, monitoring, logs, backup and the stabilisation of IT environments.

Typical process: analysis → solution design → operational stabilisation

Who this is for

  • small and mid-sized companies that need more reliable IT,
  • growing companies whose original solutions no longer scale,
  • companies with an internal IT team that lacks time or architectural capacity,
  • management teams that need a clearer view of IT risk and condition.

Even if you are not a technical person, we can explain the situation clearly and recommend what to address first.

Contact us without obligation

Situations we help with

Companies usually contact us when they already feel their IT needs more structure, order and stability.

IT is messy and depends on a few individuals

Documentation is missing, only a small number of people understand the environment and change impact is unclear.

Discuss your situation →

The company is growing and the original setup no longer fits

We help prepare infrastructure for more users, more systems and higher reliability requirements.

How we approach it →

Technical issues and incidents keep returning

When the same problems come back again and again, we help remove the underlying causes and stabilise operations.

Our services →

What we do for companies

Infrastructure design and improvement

We design IT environments to be clear, secure, stable and ready for future growth.

Cloud and hybrid environments

We help design and stabilise AWS, Azure and GCP environments including their integration with existing infrastructure.

Monitoring, logs, backup and recovery

We set up the operational areas that help companies detect issues early and recover after incidents.

How cooperation works

1) We first understand your situation

We identify what works today, what does not, where the risks are and what is truly important for your organisation.

2) We propose a clear next step

We prepare recommendations and a solution outline that both management and the IT team can work with.

3) We help stabilise the environment

Where it makes sense, we also help implement the measures needed to make IT more reliable and ready for change.

Not sure whether this is relevant for you?

Describe your situation briefly and we will tell you whether we can help and what the next sensible step would be.

Contact WOV Tech

meta.title: Articles | WOV Tech
meta.description: Practical articles and recommendations on IT infrastructure design and stabilisation. Monitoring, logs, backup, NTP, access, change processes and more.

articles.body:
Articles and recommendations on IT infrastructure design and stabilisation

This page contains practical articles and checklists that help companies navigate topics such as monitoring, logs, backup, access, change processes and core infrastructure design principles.

In short: the content helps you understand the context, name the problem more precisely and decide what is worth addressing first.

When articles are the right starting point

  • when you do not yet know where the real problem is,
  • when you want a structured overview of key topics,
  • when you are preparing for a consultation or internal discussion,
  • when you want to understand what truly affects operations in IT.

If you are dealing with a specific problem and do not want to work through it alone, contact us and we will help define the next step.

Discuss your situation

Core infrastructure design topics

Articles that help explain the important areas of IT environment design and operations.

  • Monitoring vs. logs
  • Backups and recovery
  • NTP: why correct time is critical
  • Incidents and changes
  • Access and identity
  • Networking in infrastructure design
  • Documentation and runbooks

Checklists for selected areas

Practical checklists that help verify whether the core infrastructure areas are covered correctly.

  • DDI (DNS/DHCP/IPAM)
  • NTP
  • Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP)
  • Monitoring
  • Log management
  • Backup

How to use these articles

1) Start with the topic that concerns you most

For example monitoring, backup, access or change management in infrastructure.

2) Build a basic overview

The goal is not to know everything in detail, but to understand where the problem may be and what affects operations.

3) Reach out when needed

If it is getting too complex or you are dealing with a specific situation, we will gladly help with the next step.

Looking for an answer to a specific problem?

If you are not sure which article is relevant or you are already dealing with a concrete situation in your company, write to us. We will help you orient yourself faster and suggest the next step.

Contact WOV Tech

meta.title: Contact | WOV Tech
meta.description: Contact WOV Tech. We help small and mid-sized companies with IT infrastructure design and stabilisation. Email, phone, company details and cooperation options.

contact.body:
Get in touch

We help small and mid-sized companies with IT infrastructure design and stabilisation. If you are dealing with unclear IT, recurring issues, migration or you want to improve core operational areas, contact us.

You do not need a technical brief prepared. A short description of the situation is enough and we will suggest the next step.

Quick contact

Email:
kontakt@wovtech.sk

Phone:
+421951112964

The easiest way to start is a short email. We will then recommend the next step.

What you can include in your message

To understand your situation faster, it helps if you include some basic context:

  • About you: company or team, role
  • Problem: what you are dealing with and why
  • Environment: on-prem / cloud / hybrid
  • Scope: size of the environment
  • Goal: assessment / design / stabilisation
  • Timing: plan or deadline

If you do not have all this information yet, that is fine. A short description is enough.

Company details

WOV Tech s. r. o.
Company ID 57413878 • Tax ID 2122704034 • VAT ID SK2122704034

Email:
kontakt@wovtech.sk

Phone:
+421951112964

What we focus on

  • infrastructure design
  • cloud and hybrid
  • monitoring, logs, backup
  • IT environment stabilisation

Careers and cooperation

If you are interested in work related to IT infrastructure, cloud, monitoring, log management or operational stabilisation, feel free to contact us even when no specific role is currently published.

We are interested both in experienced people and in motivated candidates with a solid foundation, reliable approach and a desire to understand infrastructure deeply.

Who we look for

  • people interested in infrastructure, cloud and system reliability,
  • independent technical people who think in context,
  • people with experience in areas such as Linux, networking, monitoring, logs, DNS/DHCP/IPAM, cloud or automation,
  • external partners as well as future colleagues depending on current needs.

Even if there is no open role listed, you are welcome to get in touch.

Not sure whether this is for you?

Contact us even if you cannot name the problem precisely yet. We will help you orient yourself.

meta.title: IT infrastructure design | WOV Tech
meta.description: We help small and mid-sized companies design clear, stable and secure IT infrastructure. When IT is unclear, growing without structure or preparing for change, we design the next step.

infra_design.body:
IT infrastructure design for companies that need more order, stability and confidence in IT

When IT grows step by step without a clear plan, it becomes hard to manage, hard to change and difficult to troubleshoot.

We help small and mid-sized companies design infrastructure that is clear, stable, secure and better prepared for growth, change and daily operations.

Infrastructure design is not just drawing architecture diagrams. It is about understanding the current state, naming the risks and proposing a solution that works both technically and operationally.

When this service is most useful

  • before a larger IT change,
  • during company growth or team expansion,
  • when more services are being added and visibility is being lost,
  • when documentation, monitoring, backup or clear operational rules are missing.

Even if you are not technical, we can explain the current state clearly, identify the risks and recommend what should be addressed first.

Contact us

Who this service is for

  • Small and mid-sized companies that need more reliable and transparent IT
  • Companies with an internal IT team that lacks time or broader architectural capacity
  • Companies preparing for change – migration, cloud, hybrid, modernisation or consolidation
  • Owners and management who want a better understanding of IT condition and risk

What the company gains

  • more order and visibility in infrastructure,
  • clearer decision-making around IT changes and investments,
  • lower risk of outages and mistakes in operations and change,
  • better readiness for growth and new projects,
  • a clear plan for what to address now and what later.

Common situations we help with

These are typically organisations that already feel their IT needs clearer structure and better operational setup.

IT grew over time without a clear system

When an environment expands in pieces, it often becomes unclear, with hidden dependencies and heavy reliance on individuals.

The company is preparing for change or growth

New systems, new teams, cloud or hybrid environments often require a better design than what used to be enough.

Problems keep being addressed repeatedly without removing the cause

When incidents return, the root cause is often deeper in the design of the environment, not in a single tool or server.

What the output of infrastructure design looks like

Current-state overview

We identify how the environment works today, where the weak points are and what the main operational risks look like.

Architecture proposal and next steps

We propose what to change, what to keep and which changes will bring the greatest benefit to stability and operations.

Outputs for both technical teams and management

We prepare outputs so they are useful both for technical implementation and for decisions on priorities and investments.

Depending on the situation, the output may also include:

  • architecture proposals and diagrams,
  • technical recommendations and priorities,
  • operational areas such as monitoring, logs, backup or recovery,
  • identified risks, dependencies and trade-offs.

Not sure whether infrastructure design is what you need right now?

Briefly describe what is happening in your company, what concerns you or what change you are planning. We will tell you whether we can help and what next step makes sense.

Contact WOV Tech

meta.title: Services | WOV Tech
meta.description: We help small and mid-sized companies with IT infrastructure design and stabilisation. Cloud, DDI, NTP, monitoring, log management, backup and recovery.

services.body:
Services for companies that need more stable, clearer and better prepared IT

We help small and mid-sized companies with the infrastructure areas that matter for reliable IT. The goal is not technology for its own sake, but technology that is well designed, clearly managed and usable in daily operations.

In short: we help companies set up key infrastructure areas so IT supports the business instead of slowing it down.

What you will find on this page

  • an overview of the areas we help with,
  • a short explanation of what each service solves,
  • links to detailed pages,
  • guidance on what is worth addressing first.

If you are not sure which service you need, just describe the situation briefly and we will help you orient yourself.

Contact us

Core areas we help with

Below are the service areas we most often address when designing, improving and stabilising IT infrastructure.

Infrastructure design

We help design IT environments so they are clear, secure, stable and ready for future growth.

Details →

DDI: DNS / DHCP / IPAM

DNS, DHCP and IPAM are core services that directly affect the availability of networks and applications. We focus on stability, clear ownership, security and day-to-day operability.

Details →

NTP: time infrastructure

Accurate and consistent time is essential for security, auditability and incident response. NTP is a tier-0 service with broad cascading impact when it fails.

Details →

Cloud ops: AWS / Azure / Google Cloud

Cloud operations focused on security, stability and auditability. The goal is fewer manual changes and more standards that can be maintained over time.

Details →

Infrastructure monitoring (server/device)

We design monitoring for servers and devices so that alerts lead to action instead of fatigue. The focus is clarity, prioritisation and real operational usability.

Details →

Log management (ELK/OpenSearch)

We set up log collection, processing and search for infrastructure and applications so that events are traceable and incidents can be handled quickly and consistently.

Details →

Backup & recovery

We help organisations set up backup and recovery so that recovery is predictable, regularly verified and clearly owned.

Details →

What companies most often need to solve

  • lack of visibility into how the environment is set up,
  • recurring technical problems and incidents,
  • weak monitoring, logging or backup,
  • uncertainty around cloud migration or modernisation,
  • dependency on individuals and poor documentation.

How we approach cooperation

  • Assessment – we name the risks, weak points and quick improvements
  • Design – we prepare recommendations, architecture and priorities
  • Stabilisation – we help implement measures that reduce risk and operational chaos

Pricing and scope

We do not publish fixed prices on the website because the scope depends on the size of the environment, available access, documentation quality and expected outputs.

In practice, we always work with a clearly defined scope and agreed outputs so the objective is understood from the beginning.

Where to start if you are unsure

If you want to understand specific areas better or prepare questions before a consultation, take a look at our articles and recommendations.

Go to articles

Not sure which service is the right fit?

Briefly describe what is happening in your company, what problems you are solving or what change you are planning. We will help you orient yourself and suggest the most sensible next step.

Contact WOV Tech

meta.title: Privacy Policy | WOV Tech
meta.description: Information about personal data processing on wovtech.sk.

privacy.body:
Privacy Policy

This page explains how WOV Tech s. r. o. processes personal data when you use https://wovtech.sk.

Controller

WOV Tech s. r. o.
Company ID: 57413878
Tax ID: 2122704034
VAT ID: SK2122704034
Email: kontakt@wovtech.sk

What data we process

  • data you provide by email (name, email address, message content and optionally company name),
  • technical data required for website delivery and secure operation (for example IP address in server/CDN logs).

Purposes and legal basis

  • handling enquiries and communication – legitimate interest / pre-contractual steps.
  • website security and operation – legitimate interest.

Cookies and analytics

The website is static. We do not use marketing cookies or tracking pixels. At infrastructure level we may rely on technical statistics and security mechanisms provided by CDN/DNS providers. These data are used for website security and availability.

Retention

  • we retain email communication for the period necessary to handle the enquiry and follow-up communication.
  • we retain technical logs for a reasonable period based on security and operational needs.

Your rights

  • right of access, rectification, erasure and restriction of processing
  • right to object and right to data portability where applicable
  • right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority

This language version is provided for information purposes. In case of interpretative discrepancy, the Slovak legal and contractual framework prevails unless agreed otherwise.

meta.title: Terms & Conditions | WOV Tech
meta.description: Core business terms for B2B services provided by WOV Tech s. r. o.

terms.body:
Terms & Conditions

Core terms for B2B services provided by WOV Tech s. r. o.

1. General provisions

These terms apply to B2B services, consultations and IT infrastructure design work. The exact scope, timeline, deliverables and price are agreed in an offer and confirmed by order, contract or email.

2. Order and scope

  • enquiries are handled by email at kontakt@wovtech.sk.
  • before work starts, a short introductory consultation may take place to clarify goals and scope.
  • the scope is typically defined as assessment / design / stabilisation or ongoing operational support.

3. Price and invoicing

  • pricing is agreed individually based on scope and required service level.
  • monthly retainers are invoiced monthly; project work is invoiced by milestone or agreed schedule.
  • invoice due date is normally 14 days unless agreed otherwise.

4. Cooperation, access and changes

  • the client provides the required access, cooperation and change approvals.
  • changes are implemented in a controlled way with an appropriate rollback plan depending on the nature of the intervention.

5. Delivery of outputs

  • deliverables are handed over in the agreed format and scope.
  • acceptance means written confirmation by email or no comments being raised within the agreed period.

6. Confidentiality

Information about the client’s infrastructure is confidential. A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) can be concluded on request.

7. Final provisions

Effective date: 2026-02-09. These terms may be updated; the current version is published on the website.

meta.title: Cloud ops (AWS/Azure/GCP) | WOV Tech
meta.description: Cloud operations for AWS/Azure/GCP: standards, security, access, IaC and operational stabilisation. Less manual work, more control and repeatability.

service_cloud.body:
Cloud ops: AWS / Azure / Google Cloud

Cloud operations focused on security, stability and auditability. The goal is fewer manual changes and more standards that can be maintained over time.

What we typically address

  • core standards: accounts/subscriptions, naming, tagging and access policy
  • security and perimeter protection: WAF, load balancing, segmentation and hardening
  • backup and recovery, storage management, encryption and keys
  • IaC and controlled change management (for example Terraform)
  • troubleshooting and stabilisation: performance, availability, incidents and operational risk

Related articles

  • Cloud: what to think through
  • Access & identity
  • Backups and recovery

meta.description: DDI (DNS/DHCP/IPAM): design and stabilisation of core services that affect network availability, security and operability.

service_ddi.body:
DDI: DNS / DHCP / IPAM

DNS, DHCP and IPAM are core services that directly affect the availability of networks and applications. We focus on stability, clear ownership, security and day-to-day operability.

What we typically address

  • architecture design (HLD/LLD), availability (HA) and rollback planning
  • migrations and consolidation of zones, DHCP scopes and IPAM data
  • automation and API integrations with strong change control
  • monitoring, configuration backup and operational procedures

Related articles

We set up log collection, processing and search for infrastructure and applications so that events are traceable and incidents can be handled quickly and consistently.

What we typically address

Related articles

Infrastructure monitoring (server/device)

We design monitoring for servers and devices so that alerts lead to action instead of fatigue. The focus is clarity, prioritisation and real operational usability.

What we typically address

Related articles

meta.title: NTP – time infrastructure | WOV Tech
meta.description: NTP and time infrastructure design: time sources, redundancy, drift monitoring and secure synchronisation.

service_ntp.body:
NTP: time infrastructure

Accurate and consistent time is essential for security, auditability and incident response. NTP is a tier-0 service with broad cascading impact when it fails.

What we typically address

  • time source design (redundancy, trust, on-prem / cloud / hybrid)
  • synchronisation and drift monitoring
  • NTP hardening and safe client configuration
  • procedures for incidents related to incorrect time

Related articles

  • NTP: what to think through
  • Why time is critical in infrastructure

meta.title: Backup & recovery | WOV Tech
meta.description: Backup and recovery design: what to back up, how to recover, testing and responsibilities.

service_backup.body:
Backup & recovery

We help organisations set up backup and recovery so that recovery is predictable, regularly verified and clearly owned.

What we typically address

  • backup policy (what, how often, retention)
  • recovery procedures (runbooks, responsibilities and testing)
  • ransomware resilience (immutability and separated access)
  • regular recovery tests and result evaluation

Related articles

  • Backups: what to think through
  • Backups and recovery

meta.title: Cloud: what to think through | WOV Tech
meta.description: Questions to answer before designing cloud environments (AWS/Azure/GCP): governance, access, network model, security, operability, recovery and cost.

check_cloud.body:
Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP): what to think through before design

Cloud is not just hosting. It is an operating model and a set of rules that shape security, availability and cost. Before design, clarify standards, access, change control and recovery.

Cloud provides speed, but also introduces new risk when governance is weak. The minimum baseline is clear ownership, IAM + MFA, network boundaries, centralised logs and monitoring, IaC and tested recovery.

Key questions

  • Who owns accounts/subscriptions and who approves changes?
  • How are admin/root access, MFA and audit handled?
  • Do you have clear environment separation, naming, tagging and policy baselines?
  • Are changes made via IaC and pipelines or manually?
  • How is connectivity with on-prem, DNS and egress designed?
  • Are logs and monitoring centralised and are runbooks available?
  • Do you have defined RTO/RPO and tested recovery?
  • Do you have cost allocation, budgets and basic guardrails?

Practical starting point

Start with a minimum landing zone (access, logging, policy baseline, network boundaries), then introduce IaC and controlled change management. Expand services and automation afterwards.

Related

  • Access & identity
  • Networking in infrastructure design
  • Incident & change processes
  • Backups and recovery
  • Monitoring vs logs

meta.title: DDI: what to think through | WOV Tech
meta.description: Questions before designing DDI (DNS/DHCP/IPAM): ownership, source of truth, availability, change control, monitoring, recovery and security.

check_ddi.body:
DDI (DNS/DHCP/IPAM): what to think through before design

DDI is tier-0 infrastructure: failure of DNS/DHCP/IPAM often means service outage or uncontrolled network change. Before design, clarify ownership, source of truth and operating rules.

The minimum baseline is an authoritative IPAM, controlled change management, response quality monitoring and tested recovery.

Key questions

  • Who owns DNS/DHCP/IPAM and who approves changes?
  • Where is the authoritative record of addresses, subnets, reservations and names?
  • What is the real HA model and how are failover tests executed?
  • How are internal and external DNS zones, delegations and naming designed?
  • How are changes made: manually or through API/IaC?
  • What do you monitor beyond server status: latency, response quality, saturation?
  • How are configurations backed up and recovery tested?
  • How are access rights, hardening and audit logs handled?

Practical starting point

Start by defining the source of truth (IPAM), the change process and minimum monitoring. Only then deal with migrations, consolidation and automation.

Related

  • Service: DDI
  • Networking in infrastructure design
  • Incident & change processes
  • Backups and recovery

meta.title: Log management: what to think through | WOV Tech
meta.description: Questions before designing log management: log sources, normalisation, retention, cost, sensitive data, ownership and incident/audit usage.

check_logs.body:
Log management: what to think through before design

Logs only have value when they support incident triage, auditability and operational decisions. Before design, clarify use cases, ownership and cost model.

Without clear use cases, normalisation and retention, logging becomes an expensive storage layer with little value.

Key questions

  • What do you actually need logs for: incidents, audit, forensics, performance?
  • Which sources are critical: identity, DNS, edge, systems, apps, databases?
  • Can logs be searched and correlated quickly during an incident?
  • Do you have agreed key fields such as user, host, service, request_id and severity?
  • How are operational and audit retention handled?
  • Do you know your data volumes, growth and storage/search cost?
  • How do you handle PII, masking and audit of access to logs?
  • Who owns ingest, parsing and changes for new formats?

Practical starting point

Start small but with quality: select 3–5 critical sources, agree common fields and set retention according to purpose.

Related

  • Monitoring vs logs
  • Access & identity
  • Backups and recovery

meta.title: Monitoring: what to think through | WOV Tech
meta.description: Questions to answer before designing infrastructure monitoring: service criticality, SLOs, escalations, runbooks and noise reduction.

check_monitoring.body:
Infrastructure monitoring: what to think through before design

Monitoring has value when it is clear what is critical, what availability target matters and who responds and how. Otherwise it becomes noise.

The minimum is to define SLOs, alert ownership, escalation paths and a triage runbook.

Key questions

  • Which services matter most to the business and what is the impact of failure?
  • What are the required availability and response expectations?
  • Who handles alerts and what is the escalation path?
  • Is there at least a minimum runbook for triage?
  • Do you monitor end-to-end service health or only infrastructure metrics?
  • How do you reduce noise: severity, deduplication, dependencies, maintenance windows?

Practical starting point

Start with a small set of alerts for critical services and components that you can realistically handle. Expand only once you have process and feedback.

Related

  • Incident & change processes
  • Documentation & runbooks
  • Monitoring vs logs

meta.title: NTP: what to think through | WOV Tech
meta.description: Questions before designing NTP and time infrastructure: time sources, hybrid environments, drift monitoring, change management and incident procedures.

check_ntp.body:
NTP: what to think through before design

Consistent time is the basis for authentication, certificates, logs and audit. With NTP, you need to address not only server availability, but also synchronisation quality and change control.

The minimum baseline is multiple time sources, drift monitoring, clear ownership and an incident procedure.

Key questions

  • Are primary and backup time sources clearly defined?
  • How is synchronisation between on-prem and cloud handled?
  • Do you track offset/drift and sync status, not just server reachability?
  • Who can change NTP configuration and how are changes approved?
  • Do you have a runbook for time-drift incidents?

Practical starting point

Time issues often show up indirectly through TLS, Kerberos/SSO and log correlation. That is why NTP should be treated as an operable tier-0 service.

Related

  • Infrastructure monitoring
  • Monitoring vs logs
  • Incident & change processes

meta.title: Backups: what to think through | WOV Tech
meta.description: Questions before designing backup and recovery: RPO/RTO, recovery testing, access, configuration backup, ransomware resilience and major incident planning.

check_backup.body:
Backups & recovery: what to think through before design

Backup is not the goal — predictable recovery is. This checklist helps set expectations, risks and decisions before the design starts.

A successful backup job does not mean you can recover. You need defined RPO/RTO, regularly tested recovery and separated recovery access.

Key questions

  • What data loss is acceptable (RPO) and what recovery time is acceptable (RTO)?
  • Are you backing up only data, or also configuration, keys, secrets and infra state?
  • Where is recovery access stored and is it separated from normal operational accounts?
  • Do you run regular recovery tests and measure the results?
  • What is the procedure for ransomware or a major outage?

Practical starting point

A good backup design also addresses security: separated access, auditability, protection against unwanted changes and clear recovery runbooks.

Related

  • Incident & change processes
  • Access & identity
  • Documentation & runbooks

meta.title: Documentation & runbooks | WOV Tech
meta.description: Documentation and runbooks for infrastructure: source of truth, procedures for incidents and changes, onboarding and the minimum standard for operability.

article_docs.body:
Documentation & runbooks: the minimum for operable infrastructure

Documentation and runbooks are not bureaucracy. They are practical artefacts that support stable operations, auditable change and reduced dependency on individuals.

The goal is not paperwork – the goal is fast orientation, clear decisions and repeatable procedures (triage → action → rollback → escalation).

Questions worth answering

  • Where is the source of truth (repo/wiki/CMDB) and who owns it?
  • Do you have runbooks for incidents, change and recovery?
  • Can a new engineer onboard without long shadowing?
  • Are configurations and procedures versioned?
  • Is documentation maintained and trusted by the team?

Typical impact when documentation is missing

  • Longer incidents: triage is slower and proven steps are missing.
  • Risky changes: changes happen without traceability or rollback.
  • Single point of knowledge: infrastructure depends on one or two people.
  • Poor auditability: it is hard to prove who changed what and why.

Related:

  • Incident & change processes
  • Backups and recovery
  • Access & identity

meta.title: Incident & change processes | WOV Tech
meta.description: Incident and change processes as part of operability: on-call, escalations, controlled changes, rollback and post-incident review.

article_incident_change.body:
Incident & change processes: the foundation of operable infrastructure

Infrastructure stability is not only about technology. It also depends on process: who responds to incidents, how changes are made, how rollback works and whether lessons learned are turned into action.

An incident process shortens outages through clear ownership, escalation paths and runbooks. A change process lowers risk because changes are planned, tested and have rollback.

Questions worth answering

  • Who is on-call and what does the escalation matrix look like?
  • How are production changes handled: approval, window, record and communication?
  • Are runbooks available for top incident scenarios?
  • Is rollback planning part of every risky change?
  • Do you run post-incident reviews and create concrete actions?

Why it works

  • Lower MTTR: signals from monitoring/logs lead into a known procedure.
  • Less avoidable risk: changes are reviewed and have a return path.
  • Better coordination: people know who decides and where to escalate.

Related:

  • Documentation & runbooks
  • Monitoring vs logs

meta.title: Monitoring vs logs | WOV Tech
meta.description: What monitoring solves and what logs solve – and why one fails without the other.

article_monitoring_vs_logs.body:
Monitoring vs logs: what they solve and why one fails without the other

Monitoring and logs are often mixed together. In infrastructure design they answer different questions: monitoring = what is happening, logs = why it is happening.

Monitoring tells you there is a problem and what the impact is. Logs tell you why the problem happened.

Questions worth answering

  • What is a critical service for us and what is merely nice-to-have?
  • Who responds to alerts and what reaction time is expected?
  • What is an incident and what is just information?
  • Do we have a runbook or at least a minimum triage path?
  • Can we use logs to prove the cause quickly, not only see the symptom?

Practical conclusion

  • The most common mistake is having a lot of data with no context, which creates noise and panic.
  • A good design defines a minimum set of metrics, alerts and logs that are actionable.
  • If you are starting out, it is better to have a small number of alerts you really act on than hundreds you ignore.

Related:

  • Infrastructure monitoring
  • Log management
  • Incident & change processes

meta.title: Networking in infrastructure design | WOV Tech
meta.description: Networking in infrastructure design: segmentation, trust boundaries, routing, access and the minimum standard for operability.

article_networking.body:
Networking in infrastructure design: what must be clear

Networking is not a separate topic only for network engineers. It is a shared layer that directly affects availability, security and incident response.

Segmentation, firewall rules, access model and routing (on-prem ↔ cloud) must be named and documented – otherwise both risk and incident time grow.

Questions worth answering

  • What is the segmentation model and where are the trust boundaries?
  • Where are firewall boundaries, who owns them and what is the change process?
  • How is access handled (VPN/bastion/SSO, least privilege, time-limited access)?
  • How does routing work between on-prem and cloud?
  • Can you debug the network quickly (flow logs, trace and a standard triage procedure)?

Typical risks

  • Long incidents: without clear boundaries, triage becomes guesswork.
  • Security risk: temporary rules and exceptions stay forever.
  • Unclear ownership: routing/firewall/DNS issues bounce between teams.

Related:

  • DDI
  • Cloud ops
  • Access & identity

meta.title: NTP – why time is critical | WOV Tech
meta.description: Why NTP is critical for security, auditability and troubleshooting. Questions and the minimum standard for time infrastructure design.

article_ntp_why.body:
NTP: why time is critical in infrastructure

Time synchronisation is a tier-0 capability: it directly affects security, auditability and the ability to troubleshoot incidents quickly.

Incorrect time breaks authentication, certificates and log correlation – and then incidents are investigated blind.

Questions worth answering

  • What is the primary time source and what is the backup if it fails?
  • Which systems are time-sensitive (SSO/auth, certificates, logs, databases)?
  • How do you monitor synchronisation (offset/drift), not just NTP reachability?
  • How is NTP handled in hybrid environments and where are trust boundaries?
  • Do you have a documented procedure for NTP incidents?

Typical impact of incorrect time

  • Longer triage: logs do not line up and event correlation is unreliable.
  • Security failures: issues with tokens, Kerberos/LDAP and certificate validity.
  • Audit and forensics: timelines become untrustworthy.

Related:

  • Infrastructure monitoring
  • NTP: what to think through
  • Monitoring vs logs

meta.title: Access & identity (IAM) | WOV Tech
meta.description: Access and identity (IAM) in infrastructure design: governance, auditability, least privilege, account management and secure supplier access.

article_access_identity.body:
Access & identity (IAM): auditability and control in infrastructure

IAM is a foundation for both security and operations. If it is not clear who has which permissions, for how long and how it is controlled, the infrastructure remains unauditable and risky.

Without MFA, audit logs and the removal of shared access, changes are unauditable and incidents become more expensive.

Questions worth answering

  • Who has admin/root and where are the break-glass accounts?
  • How are permissions created and removed (joiner/mover/leaver)?
  • Are roles designed according to responsibilities and least privilege?
  • Is MFA mandatory for privileged roles, VPN and admin consoles?
  • Do you have centralised audit logs and can you find them during incidents?
  • Where are secrets stored and who rotates them?
  • Is supplier access time-limited and regularly reviewed?

Typical real-world risks

  • Shared accounts and passwords: no attribution of change is possible.
  • Permanent admin rights: privileged roles are default and never expire.
  • No periodic review: permissions remain unchanged for years.
  • Secrets in repositories: keys live in code without rotation or ownership.

Related:

  • Backups & recovery
  • Cloud ops
  • Incident & change processes

meta.title: Backups and recovery | WOV Tech
meta.description: Backup & recovery: what to think through so backups actually help during an incident.

article_backup.body:
Backups and recovery: what companies think they have vs. what they can really restore

A backup is not security. Without recovery it is just a stored file. In infrastructure design, the key question is what can be restored and how fast.

A working backup is measured by recovery capability, not by a green job status.

Questions worth answering

  • What exactly are we backing up (data, configuration, infra state, secrets)?
  • What can we restore automatically and what still requires manual improvisation?
  • What are the RPO/RTO values for each service?
  • When did we last test recovery and what was the result?
  • Where are recovery credentials and keys stored?

Typical risks in practice

  • Backup without recovery: backups exist, but restore was never tested.
  • Shared access: the backup platform uses the same accounts as production.
  • Identity not protected: IAM, AD or cloud accounts are missing from recovery scope.
  • No runbook: recovery becomes improvisation during incidents.

Related:

  • Service: Backup & recovery
  • Access & identity
